name: Caro-Kann Defense
description: A solid, reliable defense against 1.e4. Black prepares d5 without locking in the light-squared bishop, avoiding the main drawback of the French Defense.

moves.commentary: Center.
The Caro-Kann! They're preparing d5, but unlike the French, their light bishop won't get stuck. Solid and reliable.
Take the full center while you can.
They challenge e4. This is the whole point of c6.
Defend e4 with the knight.
They capture, releasing the tension. Now you recapture and get a nice centralized piece.
Strong centralized knight. You're already standing well.
The Classical — they get the bishop out BEFORE playing e6. This is why they played c6 instead of e6. Smart.
Attack the bishop — force it to choose a square.
It retreats to g6. Not ideal but safe enough.
Push! You're threatening h5 to trap or harass the bishop. This pawn march is a key weapon in the Classical Caro-Kann.
They prevent h5 and give the bishop a retreat on h7 if needed.
Develop your second knight. You've got more space and active pieces.
They develop to d7, planning Ngf6 next for a solid setup.
Offer to trade light-squared bishops. If they trade, you open the h-file toward their king. If they don't, you have a space advantage. Win-win.
Center.
Caro-Kann.
Full center.
They challenge.
The Advance — push forward and grab space. But now they know exactly where to hit you.
Bishop comes out first, before e6 locks it in. That's the Caro-Kann advantage over the French.
Develop and support your center.
Now they play e6, but the bishop is already free. Well played by them.
Develop and prepare to castle. Steady does it.
They attack d4 — the base of your pawn chain. You knew this was coming.
Develop and support d4. Every piece helps defend the chain.
They're flexible — this knight might go to c5, b6, or support f6 to break with f6.
Castle and complete your development. You're solid.
They develop with options — f5, g6, c6. A maneuvering battle ahead. You have space, they have a solid position. Use your space wisely.
Center.
Caro-Kann.
Full center.
They challenge.
The Exchange — simplify the center. Some say it's boring. You'll prove them wrong.
They recapture. Notice the open c-file — that's their highway for counterplay.
Active bishop aiming at the kingside. You want to use your slight initiative.
They develop and eye d4.
Lock down d4 and prevent any Nb4 tricks.
They develop naturally.
Get this bishop outside the pawn chain. Your plan: minority attack on the queenside with a4-a5-b4.
They develop actively — ready to pin your f3 knight once it comes out.
Attack b7 and d5 simultaneously. Put them under pressure early.
They defend b7. Solid but passive. You've got a small but lasting edge — keep the pressure on.
Center.
Caro-Kann.
Full center.
They challenge.
Exchange.
They recapture.
The Panov-Botvinnik Attack! You immediately go after their d5 pawn. This transforms into an IQP (Isolated Queen Pawn) position — tons of dynamic play.
They defend d5 and develop.
Pile on d5 — you want to force the issue in the center.
They fortify d5. Solid.
Develop. Every piece is heading toward the action.
They develop and prepare to castle.
Trade! Now YOU have an isolated d-pawn. Sounds bad? It's not — that pawn gives you open lines and active piece play.
They grab the center with the knight. Strong post for them.
Aim at the kingside. Your plan: use the open lines around the IQP to attack their king. They'll try to blockade and trade down. A classic battle.
